metadata.working_title: Deep-learning-guided design of 5'UTRs for efficient mRNA-delivered gene editing

abstract_sketch.problem: mRNA therapeutics require efficient translation of the encoded protein, but systematic methods to optimize the 5'UTR for a given application are lacking.
abstract_sketch.gap: Prior massively parallel reporter assay (MPRA) models of the 5'UTR have been trained in a single cell type on fixed-end libraries and have not been validated for guiding the design of therapeutically relevant cargos such as mRNA-encoded gene editors.
abstract_sketch.approach: We perform polysome-profiling MPRA on randomized 5'UTR libraries in three cell types (HEK293T, T cells, HepG2), train convolutional and k-mer models (Optimus 5-Prime, Optimus 5-Prime(25)), and use gradient-based Fast SeqProp and generative Deep Exploration Networks (DENs) to design 50nt and 25nt 5'UTRs de novo.
abstract_sketch.key_results: MRLs are highly correlated across cell lines (r^2 = 0.837-0.870 for HEK293T vs T cells, 0.847-0.871 vs HepG2). Optimus 5-Prime achieves r^2 = 0.937 on held-out HEK293T data. DEN-designed 5'UTRs drive absolute editing efficiencies above 40% for TGFBR2 and above 80% for PDCD1 megaTALs at 2 pmol mRNA; the best DEN design reaches 55.6% on TGFBR2, 18-33% higher than the best natural control LAMA5, while the same sequence behaves differently on PDCD1 (80.8%), where VAT1 performs best (91.4%).
abstract_sketch.impact: Model-guided design of 5'UTRs is a practical route to tuning mRNA therapeutic potency, but cargo- and cell-type-specific effects argue for empirical validation in the deployment context.

sections.title: Introduction
content_bullets: mRNA therapeutics have expanded from vaccines to gene editing, but translation efficiency depends on cis-regulatory elements that remain hand-tuned.
The 5'UTR is a key determinant of translation and is amenable to synthetic design.
MPRAs coupled to deep learning have produced predictive models of 5'UTR function (Optimus 5-Prime) but have been limited to one cell type and fixed-end libraries.
Generative design of 5'UTRs with gradient descent (Fast SeqProp) and generative networks (DENs) is promising but has not been validated for functional mRNA cargos beyond reporters.

Contributions: (1) cross-cell-line MPRA + models, (2) new random-end libraries and Optimus 5-Prime(25), (3) designed 5'UTRs that drive strong mRNA-delivered megaTAL editing at two loci in two cell lines.

subsections.title: Polysome-profiling MPRA across cell lines reveals correlated 5'UTR activity
content_bullets: Random 50nt 5'UTR library profiled in HEK293T, primary T cells, and HepG2.
r^2 = 0.837-0.870 (HEK293T vs T cells), 0.847-0.871 (HEK293T vs HepG2); replicate r^2 = 0.938 (HEK293T), 0.814 (T cells).
Optimus 5-Prime trained on HEK293T achieves r^2 = 0.937 on held-out HEK293T, 0.841 on T cells, 0.840 on HepG2.

subsections.title: Designing high-MRL 50nt 5'UTRs with Fast SeqProp and DENs
content_bullets: Controls: 8 sequences from prior polysome-profiling MPRA, including 4 low/medium and 4 top-0.02% MRL; plus beta-globin-like natural UTRs (top ~20% among ~17,000 short endogenous).
k-mer validator test set r = 0.5213; designed-sequence predictions within top 1% of the distribution.
DENs trained to maximize Optimus-predicted MRL while enforcing diversity (Supplementary Figure 7).
In K562 megaTAL assay, editing increases with dose; several designs exceed 40% for TGFBR2 and 80% for PDCD1 at 2 pmol mRNA.

subsections.title: Predicting and designing 25nt fully-variable 5'UTRs
content_bullets: Random-end 25nt MPRA: inter-replicate r^2 = 0.692 for top-20,000 by coverage (13C,D); 0.844 for top 2,000; lower than fixed-end 50nt (r^2 = 0.938).
Existing Optimus 5-Prime models fail to transfer: r^2 = 0.564 (50nt), r^2 = 0.600 (25-100nt) on the 25nt data.
New Optimus 5-Prime(25) trained on random-end 25nt library achieves r^2 = 0.806 on held-out set (Figure 3E).
Polypyrimidine-tract position (pos 1 vs >=2) correlates with MRL, Mann-Whitney U with Bonferroni; p < 10^-20, p < 10^-100.

subsections.title: Designed 5'UTRs drive strong mRNA-delivered megaTAL editing
content_bullets: Best DEN-designed 50nt 5'UTR improves absolute TGFBR2 editing to 55.6% at 2 pmol mRNA, 18-33% higher than LAMA5 (Figure 4C).
50% of Fast SeqProp-generated sequences show lower editing despite high predicted MRL.
Same DEN design yields 80.8% editing on PDCD1 (comparable to Strong Kozak), while VAT1 reaches 91.4% (Figure 4D, Supplementary Figure 23).
Most designs match or exceed performance of 5'UTRs from the top 0.02% of the random MPRA libraries (Figure 2C, 4B); one design raises TGFBR2 editing up to 50% higher than all controls.

sections.title: Methods
content_bullets: Polysome profiling MPRA in HEK293T, T cells, and HepG2 with random-end 50nt and 25nt libraries assembled by NEBuilder HiFi with primers Bri036_T7_N50_ATG / Bri043_T7_N25_ATG.
RNA extraction, RT with UMI-containing primer, template switching, qPCR, MiSeq (25% PhiX); reads filtered to top 20,000 / 2,000 by coverage for analysis.
Optimus 5-Prime CNN architecture (retrained on HEK293T MPRA); Optimus 5-Prime(25) for random-end 25nt library with held-out performance r^2 = 0.806.
Fast SeqProp: gradient-based one-hot relaxation; optional VAE regularization; k-mer validator with r = 0.5213 (50nt) and r = 0.4094 (25nt).

Deep Exploration Networks (DENs): 100-d latent -> 50x4 or 25x4 logits; trained to maximize Optimus-predicted MRL and minimize pairwise sequence similarity. Inverse-regression mode with target MRLs 2, 3.5, 5, 6.5.
VAE training sets: 5,000 train / 1,000 test from top-10,000 by MRL in 50nt (coverage > 2,000 reads) and 25nt (coverage > 500 reads) libraries.
Environment: Python 3, keras 2.2, tensorflow 1.15.

megaTAL gene editing: 2 pmol-0.25 pmol mRNA electroporated in duplicate into 100,000 K562 or HepG2 cells via Lonza 4D Nucleofector 96-well; readout by PCR1 (30 cycles, gene-specific primers with Illumina overhangs) then deep sequencing.

sections.title: Discussion
content_bullets: Cross-cell-line consistency of MRL supports model transferability, but cargo- and locus-specific deviations argue against naive MRL-only design.
DEN-based design outperforms gradient-based Fast SeqProp in functional assays at the best operating point, but ~50% of SeqProp designs under-perform their predictions.
Transfer from fixed-end 50nt models to 25nt fully-variable UTRs is poor; library-matched models (Optimus 5-Prime(25)) are necessary.
Limitations: single dose range, two targets, two cell lines; predicted MRL is not a perfect proxy for editing efficiency for all UTR-cargo pairs.
Implications for mRNA therapeutics: cargo-specific empirical validation should remain part of the design loop.

table_plan.title: Cross-cell-line MRL correlations from polysome-profiling MPRA
table_plan.caption_sketch: Coefficients of determination between cell lines, between replicates, and between model predictions and held-out HEK293T/T cell/HepG2 MRL.

table_plan.title: Model performance on the random-end 25nt 5'UTR library
table_plan.caption_sketch: Previously developed Optimus 5-Prime models fail to transfer to 25nt fully-variable UTRs; Optimus 5-Prime(25) achieves r^2 = 0.806.

table_plan.title: Absolute megaTAL editing efficiency at 2 pmol mRNA in K562
table_plan.caption_sketch: Best DEN-designed 5'UTR for TGFBR2 (55.6%) improves over LAMA5 by 18-33%; same UTR yields 80.8% on PDCD1 while VAT1 reaches 91.4%.
